title: Fennel infusion for menstrual cramps
subtitle: An antispasmodic cup for period pain
excerpt: A hot infusion of crushed fennel seeds for menstrual cramping. Fennel's antispasmodic action on smooth muscle, the same action that eases gut cramps, also applies to the uterine muscle. One of the oldest traditional herbs for dysmenorrhoea in the western and Mediterranean tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), fennel entry, carminative and antispasmodic action, emmenagogue properties. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), fennel for female complaints. EMA HMPC monograph on Foeniculum vulgare (2008), acknowledges traditional use for mild spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints; the dysmenorrhoea application is traditional rather than EMA-confirmed at this time. Ozgoli G. et al. (2007), comparison of fennel with mefenamic acid for primary dysmenorrhoea, small Iranian RCT finding comparable efficacy at 30-minute onset.

content.text: A cup of fennel seed tea for period cramps. The same
action that fennel has on the gut also works on the womb muscle. That eases the cramping behind

. Old herbals list fennel for menstrual complaints. A small Iranian trial in the 2000s found fennel extract worked about as well as standard pain relief at the 30-minute mark. The brew is the same as the digestive fennel cup. A teaspoon of lightly crushed seeds in a lidded pot, 10 minutes' steep, drunk slowly at the first sign of cramping.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy: skip strong daily fennel during pregnancy. A cup of fennel tea after a meal is at the low end. Cooking with the seeds is fine. Talk to a herbalist or midwife about regular use.

Allergy: fennel is in the carrot family, alongside celery, parsley, and coriander. Skip if you react to any of those plants.

Hormone-sensitive conditions: fennel has mild hormone-like action. Talk to a herbalist or doctor before regular use if you have an oestrogen-sensitive condition, such as some breast cancers, womb fibroids, or growths of the womb lining outside the womb.

See a doctor for period pain that gets worse over several cycles, for periods that are unusually heavy, for pain outside the period, or for pain that does not ease with normal pain relief. These can be signs of conditions that need diagnosis.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Fennel seeds
items.prepNote: about 1 teaspoon, lightly crush just before brewing

content.text: Lightly crush {{fennel-seeds}} of fennel seeds in a pestle and mortar. Three or four firm presses to crack the seed coat. Place in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over the seeds and lid immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain and drink slowly, ideally with a hot water bottle against the lower belly. The cramp-easing from the tea and the warmth on the outside work faster together than either on its own.

One cup at the first sign of cramping. Up to three cups over the first 24 hours of painful cramping, morning, midday, and evening. Continue for the first two to three days of the period if cramping is persistent. For the fennel cup to be most effective, start at the very beginning of cramping rather than waiting until the pain is severe.
When NOT to use this remedy

Avoid therapeutic-dose fennel preparations in pregnancy. Skip if you have a known Apiaceae allergy. Discuss with a herbalist or gynaecologist if you have an oestrogen-sensitive condition. See your doctor for progressive worsening of period pain, unusually heavy bleeding, or pain outside of the menstrual period.
